meta.title: Colorado Adaptive Reuse + Downtown Redevelopment — Reference Guide
meta.purpose: Reference guide + Colorado examples for the four most common downtown redevelopment patterns: (1) hotel/motel-to-residential conversion, (2) office-to-residential conversion, (3) surface parking lot infill, (4) underutilized commercial parcel redevelopment. Each pattern has unique financing, regulatory, and physical considerations; this file captures what a LIHTC dev needs to evaluate fit before committing pre-development capital.
meta.important_disclaimer: Examples cited below are public-record precedents intended to illustrate the pattern, not endorsements. Cost-per-unit + timeline estimates are typical ranges from CHFA + Novogradac case studies, not specific to any project; actual costs vary 30-50% by structure age, mechanical-system state, asbestos / lead presence, and local code requirements.

hotel_motel_to_residential.what_it_is: Converting an older hotel or motel — typically 50-150 rooms — to permanent supportive housing, rapid-rehousing units, workforce single-room-occupancy (SRO), or studio apartments. Pioneered during COVID-era HUD CARES Act ESG funding (Project Homekey in CA + Project Turnkey-style ports nationally).
best_targets: Older limited-service hotels (Days Inn, Quality Inn, Motel 6 vintage 1985-2005) on commercial strip corridors
Downtown historic hotels with rich masonry envelope but obsolete operations
Extended-stay properties (Residence Inn vintage) — already have kitchenettes
key_pros: Already entitled for residential or transient occupancy — code change usually minor
Parking already provided — no new lot needed
Faster to delivery than new construction (24 mo vs 30+ mo)
key_cons: Bathroom + plumbing rough-in often non-standard (centered drains, undersized sinks)
Unit sizes typically 250-450 sf — limits to studio + 1BR product
Lead paint + asbestos remediation common for pre-1980 properties
Fire-life-safety code (single-exit limitation) may require new exits
financing_tools: 4% LIHTC + state credit (most common — eligible basis is rehab cost minus land)
Prop 123 grant for acquisition (DOH AHF program eligible)
Local URA TIF gap (if site is in plan area)
Historic Tax Credit (20% federal + 25% CO state) — if structure is NR-listed or eligible
colorado_examples: Best Western (Denver — Aurora Hill) → 50-unit PSH (Colorado Coalition for the Homeless, 2023)
Holiday Inn (Glenwood Springs) → workforce apartments (Garfield County Housing Authority, 2024)
Downtown Pueblo historic hotel projects (Pueblo URA partnered)
Multiple Project Turnkey-style conversions in 2021-2023 funded via HUD ESG-CV

references.name: Urban Land Institute Hotel-to-Housing Conversion Study (2023)

office_to_residential.what_it_is: Converting Class B/C office buildings — typically pre-1995 vintage with 30%+ vacancy — to residential. The 'office apocalypse' post-COVID created surplus inventory in CBDs nationwide. Denver, Colorado Springs, and Boulder all have significant Class B/C downtown office surplus.
best_targets: Pre-1995 office buildings with deep floor plates (60-100 ft deep)
Buildings with operable windows + reasonable column spacing
Buildings with vacancy > 40% (motivated owner — workable acquisition price)
key_pros: Eligible for 30% federal Historic Tax Credit if NR-listed (~25% of pre-1955 CBD stock qualifies)
Stacks with LIHTC 4% + CO state credit + HTC for very deep cap-stack
key_cons: Floor plate depth often > 35 ft — requires interior light wells, expensive
Bathroom + kitchen rough-in non-existent — full MEP from scratch
HVAC central plant rarely scales to residential load (often replace entirely)
Cost-per-unit often equals or exceeds ground-up new construction
financing_tools: 4% LIHTC + state credit + Historic Tax Credit (federal 20% + CO state 25%)
PAB allocation (4% bond pool — Denver gets significant statewide pool)
URA TIF (commonly underwritten to support cap-stack gap)
EB-5 (occasionally used for the equity layer)
colorado_examples: Tabor Center conversion plans (Denver — multiple developer pursuits 2024-2025)
Holly Park Tower (Denver — converted 2024 with mix of market + affordable)
Downtown Colorado Springs Class B office conversions (Pikes Peak Community + Polestar, 2023-2024)

surface_parking_infill.what_it_is: Building residential on existing surface parking lots — typically owned by municipalities, transit agencies, or commercial property owners. Most common in CBDs, near transit stations, and on the edge of mixed-use districts. Can be the cheapest 'land' for LIHTC because the lot is functionally underutilized.
best_targets: Municipal-owned surface lots near downtown + transit
RTD park-and-ride lots underused on weekdays (TOD conversion)
Big-box retail surface lots (Walmart, Target front-of-store underused)
Strip mall lots > 50% empty during business hours
Underutilized employee lots from downsized office occupiers
surface_parking_infill.typical_cost_per_unit_K: Land near $0-30K (write-down or low FMV) + ground-up construction $180-280K
key_pros: Land cost often the lowest in the development pipeline (write-down or land lease)
Already entitled commercial — re-entitlement to mixed-use or residential usually fast
TOD adjacency premium when on transit lots
Municipal partner motivated to convert dead asset
key_cons: Existing easements + access agreements complicate parcel reconfiguration
Required replacement parking (or parking minimum) often kills feasibility
Stormwater requirements (replacing pervious-but-paved with structures) trigger detention infrastructure
Utility upgrades (water/sewer often undersized for residential)
Local political resistance from existing users of the lot
financing_tools: 9% LIHTC + state credit (most common — full ground-up new construction)
Public-private land-lease structure (50-99 year ground lease at $1 base + revenue share)
RTD TOD partnership (transit-station-adjacent lots have explicit RTD development program)
colorado_examples: Aurora City Center surface lot conversions (AURA + multiple LIHTC devs, 2020-2024)
RTD park-and-ride TOD conversions (Federal Center, 41st & Fox, Yale)
Boulder Junction parking lot infill (2018-2024 buildout)
Multiple Englewood + Wheat Ridge lot conversions tied to W Line + Light Rail expansions

references.name: Smart Growth America Surface Parking Reform

underutilized_commercial_parcel.what_it_is: Catch-all for vacant commercial buildings, derelict strip retail, gas stations, single-tenant office, and parcels where the existing improvement is far below highest-and-best-use. Pattern is the most heterogeneous and requires the most pre-development homework.
best_targets: Closed banks, defunct restaurants on arterial corridors
Underutilized industrial near transit (light manufacturing relocated, building vacant)
Brownfield sites with mostly-clean Phase II reports + EPA remediation grants available
Tax-delinquent parcels at county auction (occasionally — usually have title issues)
key_pros: Often available at below-market acquisition (motivated/distressed seller)
Brownfield sites have EPA grant + CO Voluntary Cleanup Program funding
Strip-mall demo to multi-story residential triples FAR utilization
Sometimes catalyzes broader corridor redevelopment momentum
key_cons: Environmental due diligence costs $25-100K (Phase I + Phase II)
Title issues (defunct corporations, heirs, tax liens) extend acquisition timeline 3-12 months
Demolition contributes nothing to eligible basis under §42 (waste)
Local entitlement may require comprehensive plan amendment + rezone (12-18 month process)
financing_tools: CO Voluntary Cleanup Program tax credit
Predevelopment loans (Mile High Community Loan Fund, Colorado Housing Fund, CHFA HTF)
colorado_examples: Multiple defunct-bank-to-housing conversions on East Colfax (Denver)
Closed gas-station infill projects (multiple — required CO Voluntary Cleanup Program participation)

site_evaluation_checklist: Pull current title commitment — confirm clean fee + owner motivated to sell
Phase I ESA — most lenders require regardless of property type
Phase II ESA if Phase I shows recognized environmental conditions (RECs)
Structural assessment for adaptive reuse — confirm load-bearing capacity for residential live load
Zoning + comprehensive plan compatibility — confirm permitted use or required entitlement path
Parking calculation — confirm requirement vs available + identify variance path
Utility capacity check (water + sewer tap fees, gas, electric)
Stormwater + drainage assessment (especially for parking-lot infill)
Transportation impact study triggers (size + ITE trip-gen estimates)

lihtc_dev_implications.why_downtown_redev_matters_for_lihtc: Downtown / urban infill sites typically combine: (1) QCT/DDA designation (basis boost), (2) TOD adjacency (CHFA QAP 3-pt boost), (3) URA partner (gap financing + land write-down), (4) Historic Tax Credit eligibility (older structures), (5) walking-distance amenities (PMA scoring boost). A well-sited downtown infill LIHTC project can stack 4-5 tools that a greenfield site cannot.
lihtc_dev_implications.biggest_risk: Pre-development cost is 2-3× greater than greenfield (Phase II ESA, structural assessment, design rounds, entitlement complexity). Without a funded pre-dev loan + strong URA / municipal partner, devs often spend $200-400K and a year just to determine the site doesn't work.
lihtc_dev_implications.how_coho_helps: The Opportunity Finder filter for 'in QCT' + 'in URA boundary' + 'has Historic District designation' surfaces the highest-stack-density sites. The Deal Calc accepts hotel/office-to-residential as a deal-type radio option and applies the §42 rehab basis treatment automatically. The HNA shows the demand signal (housing wage gap, severe burden) that justifies the pre-dev investment.
